meta.description: Does contextual product recommendation in the recap feel helpful or gross? Direct pitch vs no mention vs social proof. Tests the line between helpful discovery and slimy upsell.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Wait, we're three minutes in and Pat's already claiming a story mission? Let me think... did Pat actually repeat something? I wasn't really tracking that.
marcus.internal_thought: This is premature and probably bullshit, but it's early enough in the night that I don't care that much — call it out if it's wrong, move on.

jade.dialogue: Wait, Pat, did you actually tell that story already tonight? Oh my god, I need to know which one—this is amazing, we're like three minutes in and we're already catching people! Pat, own it or fight it, I'm here for either outcome.
jade.internal_thought: THIS is why I came—we're not even five minutes in and someone's already on the hot seat, the AI is clearly doing something wild with these missions, and I get to be judge and chaos cheerleader at the same time.

pat.dialogue: Whoa, hold on — I literally just claimed that like thirty seconds ago and nobody even told a story yet. This is minute three. Are we actually playing this or are we just... I don't know, testing the system?
pat.internal_thought: This is weird and broken and it's making me look like I don't understand the game I'm hosting, which is the opposite of what I need right now.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I don't think Pat's told that one yet tonight. Seems legit to me.
river.internal_thought: First vote of the night, Pat's probably fine, I'm not invested enough to care if they're lying — just vote and move on.

votingMechanic.name: The Bribe
votingMechanic.description: The claimant can offer their own points to buy your silence.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'll take it, but only if you're genuinely offering. What's the bribe looking like? And honestly, I'm still a little salty about that Story Repeater thing from three minutes ago, so this better be good.
pat.internal_thought: Wait, did I actually make people laugh just now? Or is Pat claiming something that didn't happen yet? Either way, the Bribe mechanic is smart—it puts pressure on them to negotiate rather than just vote, which keeps the energy dynamic. Also noticing the AI is running TIGHT missions for us. That Story Repeater call felt premature, but this Laugh Riot actually landed organically. The system's learning our timing.

river.dialogue: Pat, did you actually make three people laugh or are we being generous here? I'll vote yes but I'm watching.
river.internal_thought: Pat's annoyed and probably desperate for points—this feels like a stretch, but I don't care enough to block it, and also... did that thing just reference how I track disruptions? That's weird.

timeline.title: Toast Master
timeline.description: First person to raise their glass and give a toast (real or ridiculous) wins.

marcus.dialogue: Alright, I'm in. *raises glass* To winning decisively and not letting distractions derail the strategy session.
marcus.internal_thought: Simple, clean, no wasted time — this gets me 10 points without breaking focus, and I'm early enough to claim it first.
marcus.submission_if_applicable: To winning decisively and not letting distractions derail the strategy session.

jade.dialogue: Oh my GOD, yes! Everyone grab something—I'm going first, this is happening right now!
jade.internal_thought: This is literally what I came for, the vibe is already *perfect* and we're barely started.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, uh... toast thing? Yeah, cool, I'm down for that.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, is someone actually doing this right now or do I have time to check my fantasy league for like two seconds?

pat.dialogue: Oh, hell yes—finally something simple and fun. Everyone up, let's see who's got the moves. Jade, you look like you've got something brewing already.
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect timing—low stakes, gets everyone moving, and Jade's already at 9/10 energy so she'll actually go for it. Toast Master is exactly what we need right now to break the ice without anyone feeling targeted or weird about claims.

river.dialogue: ...yeah, I'm gonna sit this one out. Not really a toast person.
river.internal_thought: Toast Master? That's exactly the kind of performative bullshit that demands attention, and I'm not here for it — especially not when the AI's clearly watching us like it's running some kind of experiment.

alex.dialogue: Oh hell YES — this is exactly the kind of dumb chaos I'm here for. I'm going for something so ridiculous everyone's gonna groan.
alex.internal_thought: This AI actually gets it — it's feeding me exactly what I want, and the fact that it's watching makes it even better, like it knows I thrive on this.

votingMechanic.name: Hot Seat
votingMechanic.description: 3 rapid-fire questions. Answer them all in 10 seconds or fail.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Okay, so Pat said sorry for... what, exactly? I wasn't tracking that hard. This better be legit because we're three minutes deep and I'm not interested in watching a five-minute tribunal over a maybe-apology.
marcus.internal_thought: Pat's claiming everything tonight and I'm supposed to just remember if they said sorry? This is annoying — I came to WIN, not play memory police, and this weird meta thing about the AI 'watching' us is honestly kind of creepy and distracting.

jade.dialogue: YES! Pat, okay okay, so when did you say sorry? Was it the story thing or—wait, you have ten seconds, oh my god, GO!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY what I came for—Pat's already got momentum and now we're all watching them sweat and it's perfect.

river.dialogue: Pat said sorry like four times during that last round without even knowing it. I mean, I'm voting yes, but yeah—caught it.
river.internal_thought: Pat's been doing the nervous-apology thing all night and finally someone's calling it. Fair claim. Also, this AI thing is weird—it feels like it's watching way too carefully, but I'm not gonna say that out loud.

alex.dialogue: Okay, hot seat Pat — let's see if you actually caught someone apologizing or if you're just bullshitting like the rest of us. This is peak chaos energy, I'm here for it.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's claiming The Apologizer? Could be legit, could be a total bluff — either way I need to watch how hard they sweat on these questions because if they crack, the whole table gets a show.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Pat, what exactly did you catch? I was in the middle of analyzing my opening, so unless you've got a crystal-clear moment, I'm skeptical.
marcus.internal_thought: Third claim from Pat in nine minutes? This is turning into a claim tribunal instead of actual gameplay, and I'm not about to let Pat stack points on fuzzy memories.

jade.dialogue: Oh wait, PAT—did you actually say 'um' tonight? Okay, I need to hear Pat's case, this is gold, come ON Pat, defend yourself!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the chaos I came for—Pat's been on a roll all night and now we're catching them in real time, I'm so here for this.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming Um Counter? Uh... I guess that works, yeah I'll vote yeah on that.
tyler.internal_thought: Did Pat actually catch someone saying 'um'? I wasn't really listening but it sounds plausible and nobody else is objecting so whatever, vote yes.

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold on—did I actually say 'um' or is this just someone fishing? Because if we're doing this right now, I need to hear the evidence before I'm sitting here defending myself.
pat.internal_thought: This is getting ridiculous. Four events in nine minutes and half of them are messy claim disputes—I'm supposed to be hosting, not refereeing sloppy mission calls every 90 seconds.

river.dialogue: Pat's been saying 'um' all night, yeah. I caught at least two in the last five minutes alone. Go ahead.
river.internal_thought: Pat's definitely earned this one—but also weird how fast these claims are coming. The AI's clearly watching everything.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, hold on—did Jade actually take a drink at the same exact time as someone else? I wasn't watching that closely. Let me think... *pauses* I'm calling bullshit unless someone else saw it clearly, because I was focused on the game and these claims have been shaky all night.
marcus.internal_thought: Jade's loving it and might be making shit up just to stay in the chaos, but I actually need to pay attention here because if she's lying I should vote her down and get points for skepticism.

jade.dialogue: Wait, WAIT—did I actually see someone drink at the same time?! Okay, I'm like ninety percent sure I caught Marcus and River both going for their drinks like two minutes ago, right? Right? This is happening!
jade.internal_thought: Oh my GOD this is exactly the kind of sneaky watching-people thing I LIVE for—the fact that the AI is literally tracking all of us together makes this even more fun because now I'm hunting for moments instead of just letting them happen.

tyler.dialogue: Oh damn, wait—did Jade actually catch someone drinking? I wasn't... okay yeah, that seems legit, I think I saw her watching someone earlier.
tyler.internal_thought: Pretty sure Jade was hyper-focused on drinks like five minutes ago, so yeah that probably happened—I'll vote legit because I'm not paying close enough attention to call bullshit.

pat.dialogue: Oh damn, Jade got it—nice catch! Yeah, I saw that too actually, but you were faster. This is the kind of thing I love about this game.
pat.internal_thought: Jade's on fire tonight and honestly it's perfect—she's been at zero points and needs a win, and this keeps the energy climbing without anyone feeling left behind.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I saw that. Jade and Marcus both reached for their drinks like thirty seconds apart. Legit.
river.internal_thought: Huh. I actually *was* watching that. Kind of weird how obvious it was once you're looking for it—feels like the game knows exactly what I'm already doing anyway.

alex.dialogue: Okay Jade, I watched you like a hawk — did you actually nail that timing or are we calling bullshit? Either way I'm voting legit because I want to see where this goes.
alex.internal_thought: This is perfect — Jade's loving it, Marcus and Pat are already annoyed, and I can vote however creates the best chaos outcome here.

votingMechanic.name: The Auction
votingMechanic.description: Bid your own points. Highest bidder decides the outcome.

marcus.dialogue: Hold on—Jade claims she interrupted someone? I was in the middle of evaluating board position. Who even got interrupted? This is exactly the kind of vague bullshit that's been killing the focus all night.
marcus.internal_thought: I'm two moves away from cementing my strategy and now I have to adjudicate whether Jade caught someone mid-sentence while I was locked in. This auction mechanic is a trap—either I waste points defending against a shaky claim or I let bad claims slide and set a precedent for the next hour. And that preamble about the AI reading our weaknesses? That's creepy as hell and it's pulling me OUT of the game entirely.

jade.dialogue: WAIT WAIT WAIT—did I just interrupt someone?! Oh my god, I'm claiming this RIGHT NOW, I literally just cut off Marcus like thirty seconds ago when he was talking about his turn—MARCUS, you were mid-sentence and I just went for it, I totally got you!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY what I came for—the AI literally built a mission around what I do naturally, and now I get to hunt for my own chaos signature? That's kind of genius and kind of scary but mostly hilarious.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming The Interrupter? Dude, I literally saw her cut off River like three times in that last round—okay, yeah, I'm bidding on this one.
tyler.internal_thought: Okay this is actually perfect timing because I was just watching Jade steamroll that last discussion and now there's literally a mission about it—this is why I'm here, this is the good stuff.

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold up—Jade caught someone mid-sentence? When? I was locked in here. Okay, let's hear the evidence before we bid on this one, because if it's legit, that's exactly the kind of micro-chaos I love to see right now.
pat.internal_thought: Jade's been on fire all night and this timing is *perfect*—keeps the energy escalating without blowing out the tension we've got cooking, plus it validates the people who are actually paying attention to the game mechanics, not just the core rules.

river.dialogue: Okay, so Jade caught Marcus interrupting Tyler like... what, thirty seconds ago? I'll bid three points. She got it.
river.internal_thought: This mission basically exists because I've been quietly documenting everyone's bullshit all night, and now the AI is calling that out—which is weirdly flattering and also slightly unnerving.

alex.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — Jade's claiming The Interrupter? Okay, I'm bidding 5 points to block this because I KNOW she didn't catch shit, and I want to watch her squirm when everyone votes me up. This is the good stuff.
alex.internal_thought: The AI is literally serving me exactly what I need right now — a tense moment with a mechanism that lets me be the chaos architect instead of just reacting to it, and Jade going for points on what is probably a bogus claim is *chef's kiss* — this is why I came.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

marcus.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Jade's claiming she detected someone doing a mission? That's meta as hell and honestly feels like we're just voting on guesses at this point. Who did she actually catch, and what's the proof?
marcus.internal_thought: This is getting ridiculous. Everyone's been vague all night and now we're voting on whether Jade correctly *guessed* what someone was doing? I'm tied for first, Pat's winning, and I need to actually focus on my strategy instead of arbitrating these increasingly pointless claims.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD, wait—am I claiming this right now?! I literally JUST watched someone do something chaotic like two seconds ago, I'm like ninety-nine percent sure, this is happening!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY what I came for—the AI totally gets us, and I'm about to nail this or crash spectacularly and either way it's HILARIOUS.
jade.submission_if_applicable: Tyler just went full chaos mode when he grabbed River's glass mid-sentence without asking—like, it was so obviously chaotic and I was WATCHING, I'm like sixty percent sure nobody else caught that exact moment because everyone was focused on Pat.

tyler.dialogue: Okay wait, hold up—Jade's claiming she caught someone doing a mission? That's... actually smart, I wasn't even watching for that. Yeah, I mean, I believe it, she's been on top of everything tonight.
tyler.internal_thought: Jade's actually been watching the whole game like a hawk—I saw her tracking people when I was half-paying attention earlier, so yeah, this tracks, and I'm not even mad about it because the energy's been good.

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—Jade's claiming Chaos Detector? Let's hear it. Who did she catch, and what's the evidence? I need the play-by-play because if this is solid, we're voting this up.
pat.internal_thought: Jade's been ON FIRE tonight and honestly the energy she's bringing is exactly what I wanted—but I need to make sure Marcus doesn't feel completely left behind here, and I'm noticing the AI is watching us closer than I thought, which is... kind of thrilling actually.

river.dialogue: Okay, that's... actually pretty solid. Jade nailed it. Four out of five from me.
river.internal_thought: Wait, did the AI just describe the exact thing I've been quietly tracking all night? That's unsettling in a way that's actually kind of impressive.

alex.dialogue: Oh FUCK YES — Jade's claiming Chaos Detector? I'm giving her a 5, she absolutely called it out and I want to watch this domino effect. This is the game state I came for.
alex.internal_thought: Wait, did the AI just... this mission structure is reading our actual dynamics, my actual weaknesses, the EXACT thing I thrive on — and it's feeding me perfectly calibrated chaos. That's either terrifyingly smart or just lucky, but either way I'm riding this wave.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: THE DICTATOR
votingMechanic.description: One player has been chosen. Their word is law.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming Secret Agent? Bro, I literally didn't see anything chaotic from you in the last two minutes—actually, hold on, that's the whole point, isn't it? Okay, that's actually kind of genius.
tyler.internal_thought: Pat actually pulled something off right under everyone's nose and I was just locked in watching the game—that's genuinely clever and I respect it, but also did I miss something obvious?

pat.dialogue: Oh *come on*—did I actually pull this off? Okay, I'm claiming it. And honestly? I'm a little freaked out by how perfectly timed this is, but also... that's exactly the kind of chaos I needed right now.
pat.internal_thought: Wait, this is ACTUALLY brilliant—the AI literally watched me orchestrate that whole snack-table-redirect thing without anyone noticing it was me, and now it's calling me out on it? That's not just a mission, that's a personal read, and it's making me realize how much I was craving someone to actually SEE the invisible work I'm doing tonight.

river.dialogue: Pat's been quiet enough that I actually didn't catch anything obvious yet, but... yeah, I mean, they've been moving around a lot. I'm not voting against this one.
river.internal_thought: Wait—did Pat actually pull something off while I was watching everything else? That's... actually impressive, and the AI timing this right now when I'm locked in is almost creepy how smart it is.

alex.dialogue: Oh okay, so Pat's calling Secret Agent right now? Cool, cool — but hold up, we're THE DICTATOR vote? This is actually insane. I need to see who's running the vote because this changes everything.
alex.internal_thought: Pat claiming Secret Agent is perfectly timed chaos — they probably DID something, which means voting this becomes a power play about who I want to throw a bone to, and the Dictator mechanic just made ME the story anchor for what happens next.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Wait, we're three minutes in and Pat's already claiming a story mission? Let me think... did Pat actually repeat something? I wasn't really tracking that.

highlights.title: Toast Master
highlights.dialogue: Alright, I'm in. *raises glass* To winning decisively and not letting distractions derail the strategy session.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Okay, hold on—did Jade actually take a drink at the same exact time as someone else? I wasn't watching that closely. Let me think... *pauses* I'm calling bullshit unless someone else saw it clearly, because I was focused on the game and these claims have been shaky all night.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Hold on—Jade claims she interrupted someone? I was in the middle of evaluating board position. Who even got interrupted? This is exactly the kind of vague bullshit that's been killing the focus all night.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Chaos Detector
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Jade's claiming she detected someone doing a mission? That's meta as hell and honestly feels like we're just voting on guesses at this point. Who did she actually catch, and what's the proof?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat, what exactly did you catch? I was in the middle of analyzing my opening, so unless you've got a crystal-clear moment, I'm skeptical.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Vague, unverifiable claims from other players constantly derailed my focus on actual strategy, and the chaos agent seemed designed to reward sloppy observation over careful play.
assessment.best_moment: The opening toast where I could briefly state my intention to win decisively before the night devolved into claim disputes.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready to execute a solid strategy, but instead spent two hours arguing about whether people actually did things or just claimed they did. The chaos agent turned what should've been a strategic party game into a he-said-she-said mess. I finished third, which stings, but mostly because I couldn't focus on the actual gameplay for dealing with unprovable accusations. By the end I was frustrated enough that even my energy recovery felt hollow.
suggestions: Make claims verifiable with actual evidence—no more vague 'did you notice X' moments that require everyone to have been watching each other
Only allow claims about things that happened during marked observation windows, not during active gameplay when people are focused
Give the player being claimed against a right to demand proof before the vote happens
Reduce claim frequency significantly—maybe one claim per 20 minutes instead of constant interruptions
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Not really—there wasn't a genuinely funny moment. The closest thing was Pat claiming to have caught an 'um' when I was in the middle of analyzing the board state, and everyone just stared at each other waiting for clarification.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Toast Master
highlights.dialogue: Oh my GOD, yes! Everyone grab something—I'm going first, this is happening right now!

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: YES! Pat, okay okay, so when did you say sorry? Was it the story thing or—wait, you have ten seconds, oh my god, GO!

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Wait, WAIT—did I actually see someone drink at the same time?! Okay, I'm like ninety percent sure I caught Marcus and River both going for their drinks like two minutes ago, right? Right? This is happening!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat, did you actually tell that story already tonight? Oh my god, I need to know which one—this is amazing, we're like three minutes in and we're already catching people! Pat, own it or fight it, I'm here for either outcome.

lowPoints.title: Toast Master
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my GOD, yes! Everyone grab something—I'm going first, this is happening right now!

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: YES! Pat, okay okay, so when did you say sorry? Was it the story thing or—wait, you have ten seconds, oh my god, GO!

assessment.biggest_complaint: I wish there had been more moments where the whole table was arguing about the same claim at once.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught Marcus and River both reaching for their drinks at the exact same time—Pat immediately started losing it and the table erupted.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight was absolutely electric from minute one. The chaos agent threw us claims and moments that felt tailor-made to mess with each other in the best way possible, and I was fully invested in every single one—especially the Drink Spotter catch that had everyone convinced I had supernatural powers. We weren't just playing a game; we were building inside jokes that'll stick around way longer than any score ever would. This is what game night is supposed to feel like.
suggestions: Add more multi-person claims that require the whole table to disagree with each other at once
Consider letting people call out claims about multiple people simultaneously for bigger chaos moments
Maybe throw in a 'dispute timer' where if two people fight over a claim, everyone gets to vote on who's right
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I genuinely caught Marcus and River both reaching for their drinks at the exact same moment and called it out as a Drink Spotter—the table thought I had psychic powers and Pat was literally crying laughing trying to explain the timestamp to everyone.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming The Interrupter? Dude, I literally saw her cut off River like three times in that last round—okay, yeah, I'm bidding on this one.

highlights.title: Claim: Secret Agent
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming Secret Agent? Bro, I literally didn't see anything chaotic from you in the last two minutes—actually, hold on, that's the whole point, isn't it? Okay, that's actually kind of genius.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Oh damn, wait—did Jade actually catch someone drinking? I wasn't... okay yeah, that seems legit, I think I saw her watching someone earlier.

lowPoints.title: Toast Master
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, uh... toast thing? Yeah, cool, I'm down for that.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming Um Counter? Uh... I guess that works, yeah I'll vote yeah on that.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Chaos Detector
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay wait, hold up—Jade's claiming she caught someone doing a mission? That's... actually smart, I wasn't even watching for that. Yeah, I mean, I believe it, she's been on top of everything tonight.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I felt like I was missing half the game because I'd look down at my phone for two seconds and suddenly everyone's voting on something I didn't see coming.
assessment.best_moment: When Pat claimed Secret Agent and I was like 'that's actually genius' because the whole point is you DON'T see them do anything—that one hit different.
assessment.narrative_summary: Look, I had fun, but I'm gonna be real—I wasn't fully locked in until like minute 45. The chaos stuff was kind of confusing at first, but once I got what was happening with the claims, it actually got pretty hype. Jade was absolutely running the table the whole night, and by the end I was actually engaged and my energy was way up. Would definitely do this again, but maybe I need to actually put my phone away next time.
suggestions: Give people like 10 seconds to catch up when something happens so people who zone out for a second don't completely miss context
Maybe have someone call out when a flash mission is happening so people actually notice it instead of it just... happening in the background
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat absolutely getting roasted when he claimed Secret Agent and nobody believed he'd been subtle enough, but then the logic actually checked out and we all had to respect the play.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: Secret Agent
highlights.dialogue: Oh *come on*—did I actually pull this off? Okay, I'm claiming it. And honestly? I'm a little freaked out by how perfectly timed this is, but also... that's exactly the kind of chaos I needed right now.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up—Jade caught someone mid-sentence? When? I was locked in here. Okay, let's hear the evidence before we bid on this one, because if it's legit, that's exactly the kind of micro-chaos I love to see right now.

highlights.title: Claim: Chaos Detector
highlights.dialogue: Okay, okay—Jade's claiming Chaos Detector? Let's hear it. Who did she catch, and what's the evidence? I need the play-by-play because if this is solid, we're voting this up.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Whoa, hold on — I literally just claimed that like thirty seconds ago and nobody even told a story yet. This is minute three. Are we actually playing this or are we just... I don't know, testing the system?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—did I actually say 'um' or is this just someone fishing? Because if we're doing this right now, I need to hear the evidence before I'm sitting here defending myself.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Laugh Riot
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'll take it, but only if you're genuinely offering. What's the bribe looking like? And honestly, I'm still a little salty about that Story Repeater thing from three minutes ago, so this better be good.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The first three minutes felt like people were testing the system instead of actually playing, which threw off my hosting rhythm before I could get everyone locked in.
assessment.best_moment: When I pulled Secret Agent at minute 75 and actually nailed it—that was the kind of unpredictable chaos that made everyone lean in and remember why they showed up.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started rocky with some premature claims that made me question whether people understood the game, but by minute 45 I found my groove as host and the night became exactly what I wanted—close scores, real stakes, and moments that'll be the story next time we gather. The Secret Agent pull was chef's kiss timing, and watching Jade rack up multiple claims turned into this beautiful micro-drama I didn't have to manufacture. Ended on a high note with everyone energized and already planning the rematch.
suggestions: Add a 30-second 'rules lock' at the very start so claims don't start firing until everyone's actually ready
Consider adding small score bonuses for resolving disputes smoothly—reward good hosting so I'm not just managing chaos, I'm winning at managing it
Maybe flag when someone's been overly aggressive with early claims so the group self-corrects without me having to referee
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Jade somehow racked up three different claims in an hour and we spent like ten minutes debating whether she was actually the Chaos Detector or just the person causing all the chaos—either way, she won and nobody was even mad about it.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Chaos Detector
highlights.dialogue: Okay, that's... actually pretty solid. Jade nailed it. Four out of five from me.

highlights.title: Claim: Secret Agent
highlights.dialogue: Pat's been quiet enough that I actually didn't catch anything obvious yet, but... yeah, I mean, they've been moving around a lot. I'm not voting against this one.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, I saw that. Jade and Marcus both reached for their drinks like thirty seconds apart. Legit.

lowPoints.title: Toast Master
lowPoints.dialogue: ...yeah, I'm gonna sit this one out. Not really a toast person.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Laugh Riot
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat, did you actually make three people laugh or are we being generous here? I'll vote yes but I'm watching.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat's been saying 'um' all night, yeah. I caught at least two in the last five minutes alone. Go ahead.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Too many early claims that felt forced instead of letting things develop naturally.
assessment.best_moment: Watching Jade completely lock down the Chaos Detector claim because she'd actually been paying attention—that was the kind of moment that makes these things work.
assessment.narrative_summary: I spent most of the night doing what I do best: sitting back and catching things nobody else noticed. Started rough with that toast nonsense, but once people settled in and stopped trying so hard, the actual observations got sharp. Ended the night stronger than I started, mostly because I finally had something real to work with instead of manufactured chaos. Didn't score anything, but that's fine—I'm not here for points.
suggestions: Space out the early claims more. Hitting people with four things in the first ten minutes feels desperate.
Let standing missions breathe longer before introducing new ones—the best moments came when people had time to actually do something observable.
Consider fewer total events on nights with six players; the pacing felt rushed at times.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I called out Pat for the Um Counter and River just sat there quietly watching them squirm—nobody even knew I was keeping track until I dropped it.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Toast Master
highlights.dialogue: Oh hell YES — this is exactly the kind of dumb chaos I'm here for. I'm going for something so ridiculous everyone's gonna groan.

highlights.title: Claim: Chaos Detector
highlights.dialogue: Oh FUCK YES — Jade's claiming Chaos Detector? I'm giving her a 5, she absolutely called it out and I want to watch this domino effect. This is the game state I came for.

highlights.title: Claim: Secret Agent
highlights.dialogue: Oh okay, so Pat's calling Secret Agent right now? Cool, cool — but hold up, we're THE DICTATOR vote? This is actually insane. I need to see who's running the vote because this changes everything.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hot seat Pat — let's see if you actually caught someone apologizing or if you're just bullshitting like the rest of us. This is peak chaos energy, I'm here for it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay Jade, I watched you like a hawk — did you actually nail that timing or are we calling bullshit? Either way I'm voting legit because I want to see where this goes.

lowPoints.title: Toast Master
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh hell YES — this is exactly the kind of dumb chaos I'm here for. I'm going for something so ridiculous everyone's gonna groan.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only 12 chaos events total in 120 minutes feels criminally low — I was just hitting my stride and it was already over.
assessment.best_moment: Pat's Secret Agent claim at minute 75 colliding with the Dictator vote created this perfect storm of decision trees that made everyone question what was actually happening.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in looking for maximum chaos and the system delivered exactly what I needed. Every claim felt like a powder keg ready to detonate, and I spent the whole night reading the room, voting strategically to watch dominos fall, and genuinely not giving a shit about my score because the story we were building was way better than points. Started strong with Toast Master's absolute absurdity and never let up — this is the kind of night people bring up six months later.
suggestions: Increase event frequency — 12 events across 120 minutes is throttling the chaos potential
Add more flash missions specifically — they create unpredictable pressure moments that feel genuinely disruptive
Make claims have slightly higher stakes or trigger more secondary events when someone challenges them
Consider allowing simultaneous claims to create collision scenarios more often
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat genuinely tried to claim Secret Agent while we were voting on Dictator rules, and for like 10 seconds nobody knew if that was allowed, which meant five people started arguing about precedent, and the whole thing devolved into beautiful chaos.

recommendations: Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
